prior.content_md:
**What it is.** FuelEU Maritime sets a binding, stepwise limit on the greenhouse gas (GHG) intensity of energy used on board ships of above 5,000 gross tonnage calling at EU/EEA ports. GHG intensity is measured in grams of CO₂ equivalent per megajoule of energy used on board, assessed on a well-to-wake basis covering fuel production, transport, distribution, and combustion. The limit is set against a 2020 fleet-average baseline derived from EU MRV data and tightens in six steps: −2% by 2025, −6% by 2030, −14.5% by 2035, −31% by 2040, −62% by 2045, and −80% by 2050. A separate obligation requires containerships and passenger ships to connect to on-shore power supply (OPS) while at berth at EU ports equipped with OPS infrastructure, in force from 1 January 2030 (with limited earlier requirements). A dedicated incentive applies to renewable fuels of non-biological origin (RFNBO) until 2033, and a 2% RFNBO subtarget may be triggered from 2034 depending on market uptake monitored by 2031.

**What it is not.** FuelEU Maritime is not the EU Emissions Trading System (EU ETS). EU ETS (Directive 2003/87/EC as amended by Directive (EU) 2023/959) targets emissions *quantity* through allowance surrender. FuelEU Maritime targets fuel *quality* (GHG intensity per unit of energy) through a penalty regime. Both can generate surcharge pass-throughs on the same voyage; they are additive cost layers, not alternatives.

**Why it applies to the workspace.** Ocean is the workspace's tertiary transport mode. For the cargo verticals served — particularly live events, film and TV, humanitarian, and high-value automotive — ocean shipments are used where air freight is cost-prohibitive or lead times permit. Every ocean shipment touching an EU port on a vessel above 5,000 GT sits within scope of this Regulation. The workspace procures carrier services, does not operate vessels, and therefore sits downstream of the compliance obligation. However:

- Carriers with compliance deficits face a penalty of **€2,400 per tonne of VLSFO-equivalent GHG intensity deficit** and will structure this as a freight surcharge.
- The penalty escalates by **10% for each consecutive year of non-compliance**, increasing the carrier incentive to pass costs forward aggressively in early years.
- The workspace's ability to identify which carriers are absorbing compliance costs through green fuel adoption versus passing them through as surcharges is a direct procurement margin lever.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1805, Articles 1, 2, 4, 6, 20, 23; OJ L 234, 22.9.2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/1805/oj/eng*

*Source: European Commission DG CLIMA Shipping. https://climate.ec.europa.eu/eu-action/transport-decarbonisation/reducing-emissions-shipping-sector_en*

---
